excerpt: Some limiting beliefs start before we are old enough to question them. This first part of Breaking Limiting Beliefs looks at childhood programming, failure, shame, mental health, and the old stories we mistake for truth.
body:
**Breaking Limiting Beliefs | Part 1**

Some of the beliefs holding you back may have started before you were old enough to question them.

That's the scary part.

If somebody insults you as an adult, you usually remember it. You remember the room, the tone, the look on their face, and probably exactly how annoyed you were afterward.

But when the belief starts young, it doesn't feel like a belief.

It feels like a fact.

Maybe somebody called you stupid when you were a kid.

Maybe you got laughed at for being overweight.

Maybe you were bullied and started believing you were weak.

Maybe you failed at something several times before anyone taught you that failure was supposed to be part of learning instead of proof that something was wrong with you.

Nobody had to sit you down and say, "You are a failure."

Sometimes life simply hands a kid enough bad evidence at the wrong age, and that kid builds an identity around it.

## Why I'm Starting This Series in June

This post kicks off a new series called Breaking Limiting Beliefs.

The series is about mental health, confidence, failure, money, self-image, and the stories we carry around without ever questioning where they came from.

I wanted to start it in June because June is Men's Health Month.

This series isn't only for men, but I do think the timing matters.

When people hear "health," they usually think about exercise, diet, blood pressure, annual checkups, and all the physical stuff.

All of that matters.

But mental health matters too.

And for a lot of men, talking about mental health still feels uncomfortable.

Not because men don't feel things.

Most men feel plenty.

Many of us just learned somewhere along the way that showing those feelings was risky.

So sadness becomes sarcasm.

Fear becomes anger.

Shame becomes ambition.

Loneliness becomes work.

Pain becomes, "I'm fine."

Then years later we wonder why we're stressed, disconnected, anxious, exhausted, or constantly getting in our own way.

## What a Limiting Belief Actually Is

A limiting belief isn't just a negative thought.

A negative thought says:

> "I'm having a bad day."

A limiting belief says:

> "This is who I am."

That's a huge difference.

"I failed" is a fact.

"I am a failure" is an identity.

And a lot of us accidentally turn events into identities.

A bad grade becomes "I'm stupid."

A rejection becomes "I'm not good enough."

A setback becomes "I always screw things up."

Over time, those stories start running in the background without us even realizing it.

## How Childhood Experiences Become Adult Beliefs

Kids are constantly trying to make sense of the world.

The problem is that kids don't always have enough information to make accurate conclusions.

If a child gets criticized enough, they may decide:

> "I can't do anything right."

If a child gets ignored enough, they may decide:

> "I don't matter."

If a child gets laughed at enough, they may decide:

> "It's not safe to be seen."

If a child experiences repeated failure without support or guidance, they may decide:

> "Trying is dangerous."

The child grows up.

The belief comes along for the ride.

Years later, that same person may struggle with confidence, relationships, money, career decisions, or personal growth without realizing they're still operating from a conclusion they reached decades ago.

## Why Limiting Beliefs Feel So True

Once we believe something about ourselves, our brains become surprisingly good at collecting evidence to support it.

If you believe you're a failure, your brain remembers every failure.

If you believe you're not attractive, your brain notices every rejection.

If you believe you'll never succeed, your brain highlights every setback.

At the same time, it quietly ignores evidence that doesn't fit the story.

The times you succeeded.

The times you figured things out.

The times you were stronger than you thought.

The times you kept going when quitting would have been easier.

Your brain doesn't always show you reality.

Sometimes it shows you the story it has practiced the longest.

That's why limiting beliefs don't feel like opinions.

They feel like facts.

## Familiar Is Not the Same as True

One of the most important lessons I've learned is this:

Just because a belief feels familiar doesn't mean it's true.

Read that again.

A belief can feel normal.

Comfortable.

Expected.

Predictable.

And still be completely wrong.

Some people have carried the same story for so long that they mistake it for their personality.

They say things like:

"I've always been this way."

"People like me don't do that."

"I'm not disciplined."

"I'm not smart enough."

"I'm not confident."

"I'm not successful."

But the question is:

Are those facts?

Or are they simply old stories that have never been challenged?

## The First Step to Breaking a Limiting Belief

Before you can change a belief, you have to identify it.

Not the polished version.

Not the inspirational version.

The ugly version.

The sentence you don't usually say out loud.

Maybe it's:

"I'm not good enough."

"I'm bad with money."

"I'm lazy."

"I always ruin things."

"I can't stick with anything."

"I'm not built for success."

Write it down.

Then ask yourself four questions:

1. Who taught me this?
2. What evidence do I actually have?
3. What evidence have I been ignoring?
4. What is one small action I can take that argues against this belief?

Not a massive life change.

One action.

One piece of evidence.

One small step that starts telling a different story.

## Final Thoughts

The biggest limiting beliefs often don't come from yesterday.

They come from years ago.

They come from moments we've forgotten, comments we absorbed, failures we misunderstood, and stories we accepted before we were old enough to question them.

The good news is this:

If a belief was learned, it can be questioned.

If it can be questioned, it can be weakened.

And if it can be weakened, it can stop making decisions for you.

So I'll leave you with one question:

What belief about yourself do you treat like a fact, even though you may have learned it from pain?

That's where this journey starts.

And that's where we'll continue in Part 2.
title: Your Limiting Beliefs May Have Started in Childhood
